text: The surgeon is performing a repair of a hernia at a fan and steel extraction site. The hernia is visible, and the surgeon is dissecting in the upper abdomen, just above the umbilicus, creating a wide flap. An umbilical hernia defect is present and will be reduced.

The surgeon continues lateral dissection to widen the flap, ensuring it does not taper to a triangular point at the distal extent. Care is taken to avoid tearing the peritoneum, preparing the area for placement of a wide piece of mesh.
The surgeon reduces the hernia, ensuring that the posterior elements remain intact without any holes. The hernia is reduced smoothly, and the bladder is not visibly involved in the hernia sac.

The surgeon prepares to close the defects in the fan and steel hernia repair, opting for a vertical closure of the muscles despite the transverse opening. The pressure is reduced to eight for this closure, and the surgeon begins by closing the umbilical defects and imbricating the diastatic portion of the lower midline down to the hernia.

The surgeon trims a 20x20 Versatex mesh to 20x18, adjusting the corners to fit into the MPO. The mesh is positioned with a few fixation points, and a corner of the mesh is visible protruding beyond the flap. The surgeon closes the flap, utilizing its redundancy to cover the exposed mesh corner.
Case went very well. Just about an hour and fifteen minutes of total time. Discharge same day and very happy overall with this repair. Interested in comments and suggestions. Hope you enjoyed.
